en.extract:
The Samoan myzomela (Myzomela nigriventris) is a species of passerine bird in the honeyeater family Meliphagidae that is found on the Samoan islands of Upolu, Savaiʻi and Tutuila. 
Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical mangrove forest. It frequents areas with flowers, such as gardens.  This is a small, active bird, measuring about 10–12.5 cm (3.9–4.9 in) in overall length. Males are black with a bright red head and breast. Females are brownish-olive, with scattered patches of red on the lower rump and back. Its long, curved bill is especially adapted for reaching into flowers for nectar.


Taxonomy
The Samoan myzomela was formally described in 1849 by the American naturalist Titian Peale based on a specimen collected when the United States Exploring Expedition visited the Samoan Islands in the south Pacific. Peale coined the currect binomial name Myzomela nigriventris where the specific epithet combines the Latin niger meaning "black" with venter, venris meaning "belly". The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.
The Samoan myzomela was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the more widely distributed cardinal myzomela (Myzomela cardinalis) but is now treated as a separate species based on the differences in vocalizations and the marked differences in female plumage.

The '''Samoan myzomela''' ('''''Myzomela nigriventris''''') is a species of [[passerine]] bird in the honeyeater family [[Meliphagidae]] that is found on the Samoan islands of [[Upolu]], [[Savaiʻi]] and [[Tutuila]]. 

Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical [[mangrove forest]]. It frequents areas with flowers, such as gardens.  This is a small, active bird, measuring about {{cvt|10|-|12.5|cm}} in overall length. Males are black with a bright red head and breast. Females are brownish-olive, with scattered patches of red on the lower rump and back. Its long, curved bill is especially adapted for reaching into flowers for nectar.<ref name=bow>{{cite web | last1=Higgins | first1=P.J. | last2=Christidis | first2=L. | last3=Ford | first3=H. | date=2020 | title=Samoan Myzomela (''Myzomela nigriventris''), version 1.0 | editor1-last=Billerman | editor1-first=S.M. | editor2-last=Keeney | editor2-first=B.K. | editor3-last=Rodewald | editor3-first=P.G. | editor4-last=Schulenberg | editor4-first=T.S. | work=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.carmyz4.01 | access-date=27 March 2025 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The Samoan myzomela was [[species description|formally described]] in 1849 by the American naturalist [[Titian Peale]] based on a specimen collected when the [[United States Exploring Expedition]] visited the [[Samoan Islands]] in the [[Pacific Ocean|south Pacific]]. Peale coined the currect [[binomial name]] ''Myzomela nigriventris'' where the specific epithet combines the [[Latin]] ''niger'' meaning "black" with ''venter'', ''venris'' meaning "belly".<ref>{{ cite book | last=Peale | first=Titian R. | author-link=Titian Peale | date=1848 | publication-date=1849 | title=United States Exploring Expedition during the years 1838, 1839, 1840, 1841, 1842 under the command of Charles Wilkes, U.S.N. | volume=8. Mammalia and Ornithology | location=Philadelphia | publisher=Printed by C. Sherman | page=150 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/43099796 }} This volume was later suppressed and replaced by a volume by [[John Cassin]]. For the background and publication date see: {{ cite book | last=Haskell | first=Daniel C. | date=1968 | orig-date=1942 | title=The United States Exploring Expedition, 1838-1842 and its Publications. 1844-1874 | location=New York | publisher=Greenwood Press | pages=54-60 | url=https://archive.org/details/unitedstatesexpl0000unse/page/54/mode/1up | url-access=registration }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1986 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=12 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=360 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482477 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=nigriventris | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=nigriventris | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=27 March 2025 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Honeyeaters | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/honeyeaters/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=27 March 2025 }}</ref>

The Samoan myzomela was formerly considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the more widely distributed [[cardinal myzomela]] (''Myzomela cardinalis'') but is now treated as a separate species based on the differences in vocalizations and the marked differences in female plumage.<ref name=ioc/>
